text: Hello and welcome to this presentation about the first UK use of the Enseal tissue sealer for pulmonary right upper
The patient was a fifty nine year old gentleman who had been previously diagnosed with colorectal carcinoma and had an abdominal perineal resection in twenty fourteen.
He subsequently had biopsy proven metastatic lesions in his right upper lobe and he was a current smoker of forty pack
The CT chest scan displays two nodules located in the right upper lobe of the lung.
So in this video we are showing aspects focused on demonstrating the use of the NCL tissue sealer in thoracic surgery.
The surgeon carefully separates the upper lobe branch of the superior pulmonary vein from its adventitia and the pulmonary artery at the hilum.
The surgeon uses the NCL tissue sealer to perform a combination of blunt and sharp dissection, identifying and separating the vessels from their surrounding adventitia in the thoracic cavity.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the upper lobe branch of the superior pulmonary vein from the pulmonary artery, preparing to isolate the vein.
The surgeon uses a stapler to divide the upper lobe branch of the superior pulmonary vein after it has been isolated from the pulmonary artery and adventitia.
The surgeon uses an end seal device to separate the adventitia from the pulmonary artery, employing an energy device to facilitate the dissection.
The surgeon isolates the truncus anterior and divides it using the echelon stapler, completing the separation from surrounding structures.
The surgeon uses the NCL device to dissect lymph nodes, employing a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques to separate them from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses an instrument with curved jaws to dissect station ten lymph nodes away from the mediastinum, facilitating precise separation of the lymphatic tissue.
The surgeon isolates the bronchus and removes the station eleven lymph node, facilitating further isolation of the bronchus.
A bleed from a bronchial artery is encountered and efficiently controlled using the end seal device, with the energy device visibly sealing the vessel.
The surgeon removes lymphatic tissue surrounding the bronchus, carefully dissecting it away from the bronchial structure.
The surgeon divides the recurrent branch of the pulmonary artery without using additional ligar clips, demonstrating precise control over the vessel.
The surgeon divides the bronchus and proceeds to dissect the mediastinal pleura, visually separating the right upper lobe bronchus from the posterior aspect of the chest cavity.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the posterior mediastinal pleura using the Enseal device, ensuring safe and efficient separation of the pleura from surrounding structures.
The surgeon completes the division of the horizontal fissure, separating the right upper lobe from the middle lobe of the lung.
The surgeon completes the division of the oblique fissure, ensuring a safe separation between the lobes of the lung.
